note: This expansion adds a roadmap paragraph at the start of Part 3 and brief transition sentences at the start of Parts 4 and 5. It does NOT restructure sections (the V6 restructure already front-loaded self-contradictions to Part 2). Instead it provides explicit connective narrative explaining WHY Parts 3-5 are ordered as they are and how they build on each other. The curmudgeon review (HOL-HIERARCHY) was written pre-V6 when self-contradictions were buried in old Part 4.5 — many of its structural recommendations have already been implemented by the restructure.

patches.old_text:
Part 3: Point-by-Point Review of Claimed Wins

3.1 Verdict Summary Table
patches.replacement_html:
Part 3: Point-by-Point Review of Claimed Wins

How Parts 3–5 build the case

Part 2 established that the dome's own geometry refutes its claims—no external data required. The next three parts test the model from the outside in. Part 3 examines every claimed WIN individually against published data: are the specific numbers, sources, and reasoning valid? Part 4 steps back to the meta-framework—the “Live Power” dashboard, statistical convergence claims, and site-page architecture—to test whether the ensemble is stronger than its parts. Part 5 applies the dome's own proposed binary tests under its own stated rule: “If any single test fails, dome is falsified.” The progression is deliberate: individual claims → aggregate methodology → the model's self-imposed falsification criteria. Each level is independent—failure at any one is sufficient—but together they close every escape route.

3.1 Verdict Summary Table

holes_NOT_addressed: HOL-HIERARCHY recommendation to restructure kill-shot order (Schumann before SH distances) — this was partially addressed by V6 restructure moving self-contradictions to Part 2. Full kill-shot reordering within Part 5 is a separate, larger task.

anticipated_objections.objection: The roadmap box is editorial scaffolding, not scientific content — it tells the reader how to read rather than what the evidence shows.
anticipated_objections.response: Structural framing IS part of the argument. A dome defender who reads Part 3 (individual claim failures) and stops before Part 5 (kill-shots) has encountered only the weakest form of the critique. The roadmap ensures readers understand the escalating structure. Every legal brief, scientific review, and policy document uses structural framing — omitting it is a strategic weakness the curmudgeon correctly identified.

anticipated_objections.objection: The V6 restructure already front-loaded self-contradictions (Part 2). The hierarchy problem is solved. Why add more connective tissue?
anticipated_objections.response: The V6 restructure solved the top-level ordering (self-contradictions before external falsification). But Parts 3-5 still lacked explicit connective logic. A reader navigating from the WIN table (Part 3) to the dashboard analysis (Part 4) to kill-shots (Part 5) had no signposting for why these three sections are ordered this way or how they relate. The roadmap fills that gap without duplicating the Part 2 framing.

kernel_of_truth: The curmudgeon is right that the strongest arguments (self-contradictions) should appear before weaker ones (external falsification), and that the logical hierarchy should be explicit. The V6 restructure addressed the first point; this expansion addresses the second. However, the curmudgeon overstates the weakness of external falsification arguments — Gaia parallax (elementary trigonometry on 1.8 billion stars) and GPS relativity (atomic clocks measuring real time dilation) are not easily dismissed as "measuring from the wrong frame." The dome defender's rhetorical escape ("your instruments assume the globe") sounds plausible but is scientifically empty: parallax is geometry, not a model assumption, and relativistic time dilation is a directly measurable physical effect. The external arguments are weaker than self-contradictions as a matter of ARGUMENT STRUCTURE (they invite frame-of-reference objections), not as a matter of SCIENTIFIC STRENGTH.
